---
title: "LRPITT15: Taught vs not-taught expressive vocabulary (generalisation contrast)"

date: last-modified
date-format: "YYYY-MM-DD HH:mm:ss Z"

format:
  html:
    code-fold: true
    theme: cosmo
    number-sections: true
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    code-links:
      - repo
---

::: {.callout-note}
Drafted by a LLM-based AI tool (Codex/GPT-5).
:::

{{< include _partials/_header.qmd >}}
{{< include _partials/_setup.qmd >}}
{{< include _partials/_gate_badge.qmd >}}
{{< include _partials/_key_findings.qmd >}}
{{< include _partials/_reading_guide.qmd >}}

## Overview

LRPITT15 tests whether the randomised intervention effect is larger for directly-taught than not-taught expressive vocabulary, one component of the trial's broader generalisation claim. Two Block 1 expressive vocabulary outcomes are fitted together as factorised marginals over the randomised window (t1 $\rightarrow$ t2):

- `TE` — directly-taught target words (`b1extau`, $N = 24$);
- `UE` — not-taught comparison words (`b1exnt`, $N = 12$).

The headline quantity is the difference between the two standardised probability-scale average marginal effects, $\mathrm{AME}_{\mathrm{TE}} - \mathrm{AME}_{\mathrm{UE}}$. A positive value means the intervention increased expected proportion correct more for taught expressive words than for not-taught expressive words. It does **not** by itself show that generalisation was limited: that conclusion also requires the marginal UE effect to be small against a substantively defined negligible-effect threshold. The conditional logit-coefficient difference is secondary. The receptive companion is LRPITT15b; the per-outcome models are LRPITT02 (TE) and LRPITT04 (UE).

## Structure

For child $i$ and outcome $k \in \{\mathrm{TE}, \mathrm{UE}\}$:

$$
\eta_{k,i} = \alpha_k + \tau_k G_i
           + \gamma^{\text{own}}_k\, \text{logit}_*(k_{\text{pre},i}, N_k)
           + \gamma^{A}_k\, A_{\text{std},i},
$$

with a per-outcome Beta-Binomial likelihood and dispersion $\kappa_k$. The 2×2 LKJ residual-correlation block is **off**, so the likelihood and priors factorise by outcome. This avoids the poor mixing seen in an earlier sensitivity fit but does not estimate within-child posterior covariance. Ignoring that covariance is not guaranteed to be conservative: the direction depends on the unknown sign and magnitude. The current contrast is therefore an exploratory factorised sensitivity result, not calibrated paired inference; confirmation requires a paired child-level randomisation-inference/permutation analysis, bootstrap, sandwich, or defensible dependence-model analysis.

::: {.callout-note title="Denominators and caveats"}

- Taught ($\mathrm{TE}$): $N = 24$ — documented (Burgoyne et al., 2012, Table 3).
- Not-taught ($\mathrm{UE}$): $N = 12$ — confirmed (#214; `n_trials_confirmed=True` in `measures.py`).
- The headline contrast is a difference in expected proportion correct, so it is not distorted merely because one test has 24 items and the other 12. Instrument content, difficulty and measurement precision remain different.
- A positive taught-minus-not-taught difference establishes only that the taught effect is larger. Assess whether transfer is small from the marginal UE AME against a substantively defined negligible-effect threshold.
- $n \approx 54$; the causal claim is limited to the randomised window.
- The factorised fit does not estimate within-child residual covariance. `use_residual_correlation=True` remains available as a model sensitivity but mixed poorly in the earlier fit; until a separate calibrated dependence analysis is completed, the report must not make a definitive paired generalisation claim.
- PSIS-LOO leaves out a child by summing that child's observed outcome-cell log likelihoods.

:::

![Model diagram](model_graph.png){#fig-model .lightbox fig-align="left"}

{{< include _partials/_results_joint.qmd >}}
{{< include _partials/_priors.qmd >}}
{{< include _partials/_prior_predictive.qmd >}}
{{< include _partials/_technical.qmd >}}
{{< include _partials/_footer.qmd >}}
